narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, LSAT score of 22.70, Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at the 1st decile, and your plan to attend full-time—and calculated a 64.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar from the outset. The most influential factor pushing the model toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers; this stood out starkly compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack 5th or 6th decile position, signaling potential challenges in sustaining strong academic performance. Next in importance, your LSAT score contributed toward the failure prediction by being well below the average of around 37, which the model sees as a key hurdle for bar success even for determined full-time students like you. On a more positive note, your middle family income bracket worked in your favor, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing since it aligns with many students who overcome obstacles and succeed. Overall, these top three elements—your end-of-undergrad performance ranking, LSAT, and family income—dominated the model's decision, outweighing other aspects like your lower-than-average undergrad GPA or common full-time status. While your situation reflects real hurdles many face, it's not set in stone; the model highlights areas where targeted improvement could shift outcomes for similar applicants. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the norm, seeking academic support to improve your decile rankings in any further studies, and exploring programs with bar prep resources tailored for students from middle-income backgrounds. Strengthening these could make you a stronger candidate next cycle, proving the model's prediction wrong through deliberate steps. Many students in your position have rebounded by focusing on these exact levers, so view this as actionable feedback rather than a final verdict.
